steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The student claims scat-detecting dogs produces the smallest difference between minimum and maximum densities. To weaken this claim, a choice must show that the scat-detecting dog study (Gregory A. Davidson et al.) does not have the smallest density range — specifically, that its range is larger than at least one study using a different method.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'David M. Choate et al. reported a density range of 4.65 individuals per 100 km² despite the use of helicopter surveying': This reports an isolated data point for one non-scat-dog study. It gives no comparison to the Davidson range, so it neither weakens nor supports the claim. ✗
  • Choice B — 'Rahel Sollmann et al. reported a density range substantially smaller than that reported by David M. Choate et al.': This compares two non-Davidson studies without specifying which method each used or how either compares to Davidson's scat-dog range. It does not address the claim. ✗
  • Choice C — 'Gregory A. Davidson et al. reported a density range greater than that reported by some studies that used other methods': Davidson et al. used scat-detecting dogs. If their range is greater than ranges from other-method studies, then scat-detecting dogs does not have the smallest range — directly contradicting the student's claim. ✓
  • Choice D — 'Gregory A. Davidson et al. reported a maximum density of 5.50 individuals per 100 km², which differed from that reported by some studies that used other methods': Reporting a single maximum value that "differs" from others says nothing about the range (max minus min). It does not weaken the claim about smallest range. ✗
That matches choice C — C is correct.
